news_summary: Scientists have developed a groundbreaking method to control electrons without using magnets, opening new possibilities for advanced electronic technologies.
news_summary_fr: Des scientifiques ont mis au point une méthode révolutionnaire permettant de contrôler les électrons sans recourir à des aimants, ouvrant ainsi de nouvelles perspectives pour les technologies électroniques de pointe.
news_summary_es: Los científicos han desarrollado un método revolucionario para controlar los electrones sin utilizar imanes, lo que abre nuevas posibilidades para las tecnologías electrónicas avanzadas.

articles.title: Scientists just found a way to control electrons without magnets
articles.summary: A surprising breakthrough in physics could reshape the future of computing by tapping into a strange, previously untapped property of matter. Scientists have shown that tiny atomic vibrations—called chiral phonons—can directly transfer motion to electrons, allowing them to carry information without magnets, batteries, or even electricity. This opens the door to a new field known as orbitronics, where data is processed using the orbital motion of electrons instead of traditional charge or spin.
articles.content:
As computing demands continue to surge, scientists are exploring the quantum world for smarter ways to process massive amounts of data. One promising direction is a field called orbitronics, which focuses on using the motion of electrons around an atom's nucleus, known as orbital angular momentum, to carry and store information more efficiently. Traditionally, controlling this motion has required magnetic materials such as iron, which are heavy, costly, and difficult to scale for practical devices.

A new study has now introduced a far simpler approach to generating this orbital motion in electrons. The key lies in an emerging area of physics centered on chiral phonons.

Chiral Phonons Offer a Breakthrough

For the first time, researchers demonstrated that chiral phonons can directly transfer orbital angular momentum to electrons in a non-magnetic material. This finding removes a major limitation that has long held back orbitronics.

"The generation of orbital currents traditionally necessitates the injection of charge current into specific transition metals, and many of these elements are now classified as critical materials," said Dali Sun, physicist at North Carolina State University and co-author of the study. "There are other ways to generate orbital angular momentum, but this method allows for the use of cheaper, more abundant materials."

"We don't need a magnet. We don't need a battery. We don't need to use voltage. We just need a material with chiral phonons," added Valy Vardeny, distinguished professor in the Department of Physics & Astronomy at the University of Utah and co-author of the study. "Before, it was unimaginable. Now, we've invented a new field, so to speak."

The research was led by North Carolina State University, with contributions from multiple institutions including the University of Utah, and was published on in the journal Nature Physics.

Understanding Chirality and Atomic Motion

The advance relies on how atoms are arranged and how they move inside materials. In solids, atoms form tightly packed lattice structures. In many materials such as metals, these structures are symmetrical, meaning their mirror image looks identical.

Chiral materials are different. In substances like quartz, atoms are arranged in a spiral pattern, similar to the threads of a screw. These structures have a built-in twist, either left- or right-handed, that cannot be superimposed on its mirror image. Human hands are a simple example of chirality.

Atoms in solids are not static. They vibrate in place. In symmetrical materials, this motion tends to be side-to-side. In chiral materials, the twisted structure causes atoms to move in a circular or spiral-like pattern.

How Chiral Phonons Move Energy

These vibrations can travel through a material as collective waves known as phonons. In chiral materials, these waves also follow a circular motion, forming chiral phonons. A helpful way to picture this is a crowd at a concert where one person starts swaying and the motion spreads through the group.

Because the atoms move in a circular path, they carry angular momentum. The researchers showed that this motion can be passed directly to electrons, giving them orbital angular momentum without relying on traditional magnetic methods.

Quartz Reveals Hidden Magnetic Effects

Electrons carry a negative charge, so magnetic fields are typically needed to influence their motion. Quartz, however, offers a surprising advantage. It is lightweight, inexpensive, and its chiral phonons generate their own internal magnetic effects.

For the first time, scientists at the University of Utah directly measured this magnetism in quartz using specialized equipment at the National High Magnetic Field Lab in Florida. By shining lasers through the material and studying how the reflected light changed in color, wavelength, etc., they confirmed that chiral phonons in quartz produce a significant magnetic field.

"Even though the material itself isn't magnetic, the existence of chiral phonons gives us these magnetic levers to pull on," said Rikard Bodin, doctoral candidate at the U and co-author of the paper. "When we talk about discovering things, like the orbital Seebeck effect -- I can't tell you that your TV is going to run on it, but it's creating more levers that we can pull on to do new things. Now that it's here, someone else can push it forward and before you know it, it's ubiquitous. That's how technology is."

Aligning Phonons to Drive Electron Flow

Under normal conditions, chiral phonons exist in a mix of left- and right-handed states with varying energy levels. To test their concept, the researchers used α-quartz, a crystal with a naturally chiral structure. By applying a magnetic field, they were able to align these phonons.

Once enough phonons were aligned, their collective motion transferred to electrons, even after the external magnetic field was removed. This produced a flow of orbital angular momentum, which the team named the orbital Seebeck effect, drawing inspiration from the spin Seebeck effect that influences electron spin.

To detect this effect, the scientists layered metals (tungsten and titanium) on top of the α-quartz. This setup converted the otherwise hidden orbital motion into an electrical signal that could be measured.

Toward More Efficient Electronics

The approach is not limited to quartz. It can also be applied to other chiral materials such as tellurium, selenium, and hybrid organic/inorganic perovskites. Compared to existing methods, it requires fewer materials and allows the orbital motion to persist much longer.

This combination of simplicity, efficiency, and scalability could make orbitronics a more practical option for future technologies, potentially leading to faster and more energy-efficient devices.

The study involved a wide collaboration of researchers from institutions including North Carolina State University, the University of Utah, Nanjing Normal University, the Air Force Research Laboratory, the University of Washington, the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, the National High Magnetic Field Laboratory, the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign, the University of South Carolina, and Pennsylvania State University.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in controlling electron motion without magnets, batteries, or electricity by using chiral phonons. This discovery opens a new field called orbitronics, which promises more efficient, scalable, and cost-effective data processing technologies. The impact is broad, potentially transforming future electronics and computing, and the article provides detailed context about the discovery, its mechanism, and implications.
articles.personality_title: Scientists control electron motion using atomic vibrations, no magnets needed
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists want to find new ways to control and use electrons to process data faster and more efficiently. Usually, this requires magnets or electric currents, which can be costly and complicated.

**What happened** – Researchers discovered that tiny vibrations inside certain materials, called chiral phonons, can move electrons without magnets or electricity. These vibrations happen in materials where atoms are arranged in a twisted, spiral pattern. Using quartz crystals, the team showed these vibrations can push electrons to carry information by themselves.

**Impact** – This discovery creates a new way to control electrons called orbitronics. It uses the natural motion of electrons around atoms instead of their charge or spin. This method uses cheaper materials like quartz and does not need magnets or batteries, which could make future devices smaller, cheaper, and more energy-efficient.

**What's next step** – Scientists will explore other materials with similar properties and try to build real devices using this method. This could lead to new kinds of computers and electronics that work faster and use less power.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists found a way to control electron motion using atomic vibrations in twisted crystals, opening a simpler path to advanced electronics without magnets or electricity.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques contrôlent le mouvement des électrons grâce aux vibrations atomiques, sans aimants
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchent de nouvelles façons de contrôler et d’utiliser les électrons pour traiter les données plus rapidement et efficacement. Habituellement, cela nécessite des aimants ou des courants électriques, ce qui peut être coûteux et compliqué.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont découvert que de minuscules vibrations dans certains matériaux, appelées phonons chiraux, peuvent déplacer les électrons sans aimants ni électricité. Ces vibrations se produisent dans des matériaux dont les atomes sont disposés en spirale. En utilisant des cristaux de quartz, l’équipe a montré que ces vibrations peuvent pousser les électrons à porter l’information par eux-mêmes.

**Impact** – Cette découverte crée une nouvelle façon de contrôler les électrons appelée orbitronique. Elle utilise le mouvement naturel des électrons autour des atomes au lieu de leur charge ou de leur spin. Cette méthode utilise des matériaux moins chers comme le quartz et ne nécessite ni aimants ni batteries, ce qui pourrait rendre les futurs appareils plus petits, moins chers et plus économes en énergie.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont étudier d’autres matériaux aux propriétés similaires et essayer de construire de vrais appareils avec cette méthode. Cela pourrait conduire à de nouveaux types d’ordinateurs et d’électroniques plus rapides et moins gourmands en énergie.

**Phrase clé** – Les scientifiques ont trouvé un moyen de contrôler le mouvement des électrons grâce aux vibrations atomiques dans des cristaux torsadés, ouvrant une voie plus simple vers l’électronique avancée sans aimants ni électricité.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos controlan el movimiento de electrones usando vibraciones atómicas, sin imanes
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos buscan nuevas formas de controlar y usar electrones para procesar datos de manera más rápida y eficiente. Normalmente, esto requiere imanes o corrientes eléctricas, lo que puede ser caro y complicado.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores descubrieron que pequeñas vibraciones dentro de ciertos materiales, llamadas fonones quirales, pueden mover electrones sin imanes ni electricidad. Estas vibraciones ocurren en materiales donde los átomos están organizados en un patrón en espiral. Usando cristales de cuarzo, el equipo mostró que estas vibraciones pueden empujar a los electrones para que transporten información por sí mismos.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento crea una nueva forma de controlar electrones llamada orbitrónica. Usa el movimiento natural de los electrones alrededor de los átomos en lugar de su carga o spin. Este método usa materiales más baratos como el cuarzo y no necesita imanes ni baterías, lo que podría hacer que los dispositivos futuros sean más pequeños, económicos y eficientes en energía.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos explorarán otros materiales con propiedades similares y tratarán de construir dispositivos reales usando este método. Esto podría llevar a nuevos tipos de computadoras y electrónicos que funcionen más rápido y consuman menos energía.

**Frase clave** – Los científicos encontraron una forma de controlar el movimiento de electrones usando vibraciones atómicas en cristales torcidos, abriendo un camino más simple hacia la electrónica avanzada sin imanes ni electricidad.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a spiraling quartz crystal lattice glowing softly with swirling, circular waves of light representing chiral phonons transferring energy to orbiting electron orbits depicted as glowing rings around the crystal, set against a simple, natural background with gentle gradients of earthy colors.
